coredata.dc:title: Effect of Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanoparticles supported on carbon quantum dot and graphitic carbon nitride for improving electrochemical hydrogen storage
coredata.dc:creator: Karkeh-Abadi, Fatemeh
Ghiyasiyan-Arani, Maryam
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
coredata.dc:description: The development of electrode materials for energy storage is an important request for providing novel active materials. Co-precipitation synthesized nanostructured magnesium zinc molybdate (Mg-Zn-Mo-O) along with graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) and carbon quantum dot (CQD) form ternary nanocomposite for applying in the hydrogen storage systems in terms of electrochemical storage mechanism. Carbon quantum dot materials produce by a novel natural source of Salix aegyptiaca through the green synthesis method. The ternary nanocomposites of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 /CQD apply as hydrogen storage material and compare with binary nanocomposites of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 and Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD in the electrochemical charge-discharge chronopotentiometry tests. The resultant discharge profiles show the maximum capacity of 202 mA h g−1 after 15 cycles for Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 /CQD composites while the maximum capacity for Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 and Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD is 250 and 162 mA h g−1. The capacity of pure Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanoparticles is 112 mA h g−1. Therefore, the resultant ternary nanocomposites are promising active materials for hydrogen storage application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposites
Molybdate
Nanostructures
G-C3N4
Hydrogen storage
Green synthesis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction pattern for (a), g-C3N4 and (b) CQD.
X-ray diffraction pattern of the prepared nanomaterials (a) Mg-Zn-Mo-O, (b) Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4, (c) Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD and (d) Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD.
The FTIR spectra in the range of 400 – 4000 cm−1 for (a) g-C3N4, (b) CQD, (c) Mg-Zn-Mo-O, (d) Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4, (e) Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD and (f) Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD.
FESEM images of the prepared samples a) Mg-Zn-Mo-O before calcination, b) Mg-Zn-Mo-O after calcination, c) Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4, d) Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD and e) Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD.
The FESEM-Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDS) micrograph and elemental map of (a) Mg-Zn-Mo-O and Mg-Zn-Mo-O /g-C3N4/CQD.
TEM and HR-TEM images for provided ternary nanocomposites of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD.
Discharge profiles for a) Mg-Zn-Mo-O, binary nanocomposites of b) Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4, c) Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD and ternary nanocomposites of d) Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD.
Electrode feedback of CV voltammograms for nominated samples.

head.ce:title: Effect of Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanoparticles supported on carbon quantum dot and graphitic carbon nitride for improving electrochemical hydrogen storage

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan P. O. Box. 87317-51167, Islamic Republic of Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P. O. Box. 87317–51167, I. R. Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of electrode materials for energy storage is an important request for providing novel active materials. Co-precipitation synthesized nanostructured magnesium zinc molybdate (Mg-Zn-Mo-O) along with graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) and carbon quantum dot (CQD) form ternary nanocomposite for applying in the hydrogen storage systems in terms of electrochemical storage mechanism. Carbon quantum dot materials produce by a novel natural source of Salix aegyptiaca through the green synthesis method. The ternary nanocomposites of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 /CQD apply as hydrogen storage material and compare with binary nanocomposites of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 and Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD in the electrochemical charge-discharge chronopotentiometry tests. The resultant discharge profiles show the maximum capacity of 202 mA h g−1 after 15 cycles for Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 /CQD composites while the maximum capacity for Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 and Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD is 250 and 162 mA h g−1. The capacity of pure Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanoparticles is 112 mA h g−1. Therefore, the resultant ternary nanocomposites are promising active materials for hydrogen storage application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para:
Unfortunately, healthy and safe weather is decreasing daily, with increasing populations, ever-developing consumption of fossil fuels, and extreme ecosystem change [1]. Thus, attention has been increased to renewable energies, especially hydrogen as clean energy and green fuel [2–4]. It is necessary for using energy hydrogen attention to preparation, storage, and application [5]. There are several methods for hydrogen storage which among these methods, the electrochemical method is the best method due to the hydrogen storage at the appropriate temperature and pressure [6]. Hydrogen storage takes place in various systems, including porous and layered materials (Silica and alumina materials, Carbon materials, porous polymers, Metal-organic framework materials, Pillared clay, metal oxide and etc.) [7]. Electrochemical hydrogen storage categorization on the basis of procedure is sorted into two avenues of (i) Adsorption based storage and (ii) Absorption based storage. Mechanism in adsorption-based systems can describe via electrochemical reactions. At working electrode, water itself is the source of protons in the electrolyte solution which leads to OH– as following electron reaction (Eq. 1) and the evolving hydrogen ions are adsorbed on the adsorbent surfaces (A) in the Eq. (2) or recombine to H2 molecules as Eqs. (3)–(5).
(1) H2O + e − → H + OH−
(2) A + H → AHads
(3) AHads + H2O + e- → H2 + OH- + A
(4) 2 H → H2
(5) AHads + AHads → H2 + 2A

The mentioned equation of (1), (3), and (4) are Volmer, Heyrovsky, and Tafel reactions represented for different stages in the electrochemical process [8]. If the freed energy of Tafel or Heyrovsky feedbacks is less than the energy generated in the hydrogen adsorption reaction, and the initiation block for one of these reactions is small sufficiently, Hads recombination occurs, and molecular hydrogen develops electrochemically. If not, the adsorbed hydrogen might migrate further into the adsorbent, occupying sites with higher adsorption energy [9].

Nanomaterials are considered in energy storage applications because of their numerous advantages [10]. In recent years, extensive efforts have been made to improve hydrogen storage based on nanotechnology. Materials with relatively high storage capacity, moderate thermolysis temperature, strong reversibility, rapid uptake and release can be used for hydrogen storage. Nevertheless, no material has yet been introduced that has all of these criteria at the same time [10].

Research has demonstrated that magnesium is a candidate for hydrogen storage due to its properties [11]. Mg is an abundant element on earth with a high theoretical hydrogen storage capacity of 7.6 wt%, and its high reversibility, cycle stability and poison-resistance render it promising for energy conversion, fuel cells and heat storage applications in the future. However, the practical problems of magnesium in hydrogen storage prevent it is expanding economic. The disadvantages of high operating temperature and sluggish (de-)hydrogenation kinetics hinder its large-scale application in expanding the hydrogen economy [12]. Researchers are trying to overcome these problems and use magnesium extensively economically; in ways such as: synthesizing composites [13], catalytic effects [11], alloying [14]. Molybdenum is one of the elements that has been considered in hydrogen and energy storage due to different oxidation states [15]. Complex molybdenum-based structures such as Bi2 (MoO4)3, CuCe2(MoO4)4, SrCe2(MoO4)4 have exhibited in hydrogen storage [16–18]. Therefore, can be synthesized new complex structures with magnesium, molybdenum, and other elements and investigated in hydrogen storage.

Carbon is an ideal material for hydrogen storage because of its structural properties such as quick adsorption and desorption, less weight than inorganic compounds, long life, and mechanical stability [19]. Carbon nanostructures consisting of graphene, graphene oxide, graphite, Carbon nanotubes [20–25], have been studied in hydrogen storage. Zero-dimensional carbon compounds (Carbon quantum dot and Graphene quantum dot) have attracted a lot of attention due to their extraordinary properties [26]. These compounds have been exploited in storage devices, thermoelectric, antibacterial, photocatalyst and bio-imaging [27–31]. Carbon quantum dots can be synthesized by the hydrothermal method, which is an environmentally friendly process [29]. Due to the high carbon content and fewer hazardous effects, carbon quantum dots have been synthesized from natural materials [32]. for example, has been used in the synthesis of carbon quantum dots sugarcane bagasse pulp [27], Cabbage [32], purslane leaves [33], Mexican Mint [34], and banana juice [35]. Very recently, graphene quantum dots containing of functional consociations have emerged as a novel principle for the functional nanomaterials based on carbon structures, and show ideal chemical and physical properties owing to their strong quantum confinement and edge effects. There are only a few reports thus far on the usage of carbon-based quantum dots for highly effective overall energy storage systems [36]. For instance, Li Ruiyi et. al. reported a hybrid of Li4Ti5O12 and nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots for use in Li-ion battery anodes [37]. The resultant graphene quantum dot-coated VO2 array grown by CVD method possesses outstanding stability for Li and Na-ion batteries reported by Dongliang Chao et al. [38]. Similarly, Jiahuan Luo and coworkers synthesized tremella-like NiCo2O4@GQDs for application in asymmetric supercapacitors [39]. The nanocomposites of S/N doped GQDs/TiNb2O7 applied in hydrogen storage through electrochemical method. The interconnected S/N-doped graphene quantum dots make the nanocomposites more electron-rich and have improved conductivity, and pyridinic nitrogen produces electrophilic active sites to facilitate hydrogen absorption on the material [40].

Among other carbon materials, graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a two-dimensional, porous, and graphite-like layered structure[41,42]. Nitrogen atoms in the structure g-C3N4 cause an increase in the surface electron density and electron exchange rate in the storage and release of hydrogen [43]. Recently, g-C3N4 has efficient properties which is applied as photocatalyst [44], water splitting [45], and reduce carbon dioxide [46]. g-C3N4 not only fascinating attributes including high surface area, chemical stability, small band gap, and low-cost synthesis methods but also a great potential as electrode material for energy storage capacitors including supercapacitors, batteries, fuel cells and hydrogen storage systems [47,48]. Therefore, graphite carbon nitride can be used as a substrate for fabricating composites with metal oxides and evaluated in hydrogen storage. On the other hand, the study on ternary systems based on carbon nanostructures has created new research potential. The ternary composites consist of g-C3N4/CQDs/CdIn2S4 [49], BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 [50], and α-Fe2O3/CQD@g-C3N4 [51], has been examined in photocatalytic research. To our knowledge, research has not been done on the use of metal oxide/g-C3N4/CQD in hydrogen storage.

Hence, in this contribution, first pure magnesium zinc molybdate (Mg-Zn-Mo-O) nanoparticles were synthesized. Afterward, we synthesized novel two and three-component nanocomposites (Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4, Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD, and Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD). Different spectroscopic methods characterized the prepared nanostructures. The application of newly introduced nanostructures for electrochemical hydrogen storage is explored. Furthermore, the green synthesis of carbon quantum dots from the Salix aegyptiaca is done using an easy and cost-effective hydrothermal method.

In the current study, the nanocomposites of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD designed for electrochemical hydrogen storage application. The principal aims are perceived following:
1)
The two-step synthesis has been applied to formation of nanostructured Mg-Zn-Mo-O by cooperation of precipitation and cation exchange method for the first time.
2)
For the first time, the carbon quantum dot was prepared from new natural source of Salix aegyptiaca through hydrothermal technique.
3)
The texture of electrode materials was prepared based on nanostructured Mg-Zn-Mo-O and resulting CQD and g-C3N4.
4)
The designed nanocomposites utilized as electrode materials in the chronopotentiometry test for comparing the electrochemical hydrogen storage efficiency of nanocomposites which provided by different component of g-C3N4, CQD or both of them.
5)
The designed nanocomposites were utilized for hydrogen storage, for initial time to obtain the purpose of storing hydrogen. We have applied these nanocomposites as new and effective agent for potential active materials.

In this study, nanostructured Mg-Zn-Mo-O were synthesized using precursor materials including magnesium nitrate hexahydrate (Merck company), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Aldrich company), sodium molybdate dihydrate (Merck company), polyvinylpyrrolidone (Aldrich company), nitric acid (Merck company), and ammonia solution (Aldrich company). Graphite carbon nitrides (g-C3N4) were prepared using 2,4,6-Triamino-1,3,5-triazine (Aldrich company). All starting materials applied as arrived without further purification. Also, carbon quantum dot (CQD) was synthesized using Salix aegyptiaca (collected from the gardens of Kashan).

To verify the synthesis of nanomaterials, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (Avaspec 2048 TEC spectroscopy device manufactured by Avantes Netherlands) of nanomaterials has been within the range of 400–4000 cm−1. XRD diffractograms were recorded by X-ray diffractometer device using Ni-filtered Cu Ka radiation (Philips-X′pertpro). X-ray diffraction (XRD pattern) in the 2θ range of 10–80° was registered using the diffractometer Model X′Pert PRO MPD PANalytical Company Made in the Netherlands. The surface morphology of the synthesized nanomaterials was examined using FESEM (TESCAN MIRA 3 XMU). The coated nanomaterials with gold were studied using an Energy Dispersive X-ray. Furthermore, metal mapping was performed to confirm the presence of carbon and nitrogen elements in the nanocomposite. A Belsorp mini II (Bel, Japan) device is used to investigate N2 adsorption and desorption. TEM images were captured on a Philips EM208 transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

The Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanoparticles were synthesized using two steps through co-precipitation and ion exchange method. First, 4 g of Na2MoO4·2H2O and 2 g Polyvinylpyrrolidone (capping agent) were dissolved in deionized water. In another beaker, 1.57 g Zn(NO3)2·6H2O salt was dissolved in deionized water. pH of this solution was adjusted to 9 by slowly adding ammonia. The homogeneous sodium molybdate solution was added to the zinc nitrate solution while stirring vigorously. Next, the HNO3 solution (0.4 M) was added dropwise to the mixture at 80 °C to produce the white colloidal suspension. The sodium zinc molybdate precursor was collected, washed many times with deionized water, and dried at 50 °C for 24 h.

In the second step, 2 g sodium zinc molybdate precursor and 0.6 g Mg(NO3)2·6H2O were dissolved in deionized water in two various beakers. The resulting white color solutions were mixed and stirred at 80 °C. The reaction stopped when the color of the colloidal solution changed to yellowish color. Finally, as-synthesized Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanoparticles were calcined at 600 °C for 2 h to remove impurities and organic compounds (Schematic. 1a).

Graphite carbon nitride nanosheet was prepared according to the previous method [52]. Briefly, 2,4,6-Triamino-1,3,5-triazine (Melamine) was calcined for 2 h at 550 °C. After calcination and cooling, the yellow color product was milled into powder (Schematic.1b).

The Carbon quantum dot was synthesized with natural material and an eco-friendly method. For the first time, Salix aegyptiaca was used as a carbon source. The milled Salix aegyptiaca (3 g) was added to 30 mL of ultrapure water. Then, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and in the oven heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling the autoclave, the brown color solution was centrifuged for 4 h at high speed. Eventually, the supernatant liquid was separated from solid particles and stored for use (Schematic. 1c).

Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 binary nanocomposites were synthesized using ultrasonication-assisted and reflux. g-C3N4 and Mg-Zn-Mo-O (weight ratio = 2:1) were dispersed in 40 mL of deionized water via ultrasonic irradiation for 20 min, followed by stirring vigorously for 5 h. The resulting suspension was refluxed for 36 h. The product was collected with filtration and dried in an oven at 45 °C for 24 h.

In order to prepare Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD nanocomposites, the pre-synthesized CQD freeze dried to form powder product. Next, CQD was dispersed in deionized water using an ultrasonic bath, and Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanoparticles were added (weight ratio = 2:1). The resultant sample was refluxed for 24 h to make a homogeneous suspension. Finally, the product was collected and dried at room temperature.
The ternary nanocomposite of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD was fabricated by combining the synthesis methods of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 and Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD in Sections 2.3.4 and 2.3.5.

The electrochemical tests including for the considered nano-materials was questioned to comparison of samples in terms of electrochemical performance for utilizing in hydrogen sorption request. The electrochemical cell gathered using 2.0 M KOH electrolyte and three-electrodes of planned nanocomposites (working), Pt (counter) and Ag/AgCl (reference) electrodes, correspondingly. Current at 1 mA was applied in the assembled cell between the working and Pt electrodes and the potential difference between the working and the Ag/AgCl electrodes was saved. In order to the performing the working electrode, the free-binder method of drop-cast was studied with deposition of active materials on the Cu foam (area = 1 cm2).

The XRD patterns of the pristine CQD and g-C3N4 display in Fig. 1(a, b). Also, the XRD pattern for Mg-Zn-Mo-O, Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4, Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD, and Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD, are shown in Fig. 2(a–d). The XRD pattern of g-C3N4 contains the characteristic peaks at 2θ of 13.16 ° and 27.61° correspond to the (001) and (002) planes (JCPDS file number. 87–1526). According to the previous research of Singh et.al. the characteristic signals at 14.0°, 17.5°, 25.2°, 27.1°, 28.9°, 31.5°, 32.1°, and 34.0° are consistent with those of MgZn(MoO4)2 which specified with # symbol in the XRD pattern [53]. The presence of other peaks (specified with * and $) related to the ZnMoO4 (JCPDS: 35-0765) and MgMoO4 (JCPDS: 44-0355). Obtaining other peaks with less intense may be due to the presence of not reacted sodium zinc molybdate precursor. Moreover, the Mg-Zn-Mo-O /CQD, Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 and Mg-Zn-Mo-O /g-C3N4/CQD composites are shown to have no peak of CQD and g-C3N4 due to low intensity of characteristic peak of CQD and g-C3N4 and overlap with characteristic peak of Mg-Zn-Mo-O.

According to the Fig. 3, the FTIR studies of the prepared nanomaterials were performed to characterization of chemical bands. The FTIR spectrum of graphite carbon nitride (Fig. 3a) exhibited a wide peak at 3180 cm−1 which corresponds to stretching vibration N-H [54]. The absorption bands from 1630 to 1240 cm−1can be assigned to the stretching vibrations C-N or CN in the g-C3N4 [55] . The peak breathing mode of tri-s-triazine is located at 804 cm−1 [54].

There are several absorption peaks in the FTIR spectrum of CQD (Fig. 3b) which is related to the functional groups on the CQD surface and chemical structure of green synthesized CQD from reaction Salix aegyptiaca. The peak located at 3396 cm−1 was corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O-H and the peak at 2937 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-H [56]. The peaks located at 1670, 1407, 1290, and 1119 cm−1 are related to the vibrational absorption band of CO [31], symmetric stretching vibration of COO− [57], stretching vibration C–OH [57], and C–O (carbonyl group) [58] respectively. The peak at 1597 cm−1 can be assigned to the C = C band (aromatic rings) [58].

The FTIR spectrum of Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanomaterial shows two peaks at 3425 and 1628 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration O-H of the adsorbed water molecules. The characteristic bands at 948 and 887 cm−1 can be ascribed to the symmetric and antisymmetric stretching of the MoO. There are coincided bands at 804 and 755 cm−1 that are related to the vibrations Mg-O-Mo and Zn-O-Mo. In addition, the peak at 443 cm−1 is related to the bending vibrations of O-Mo-O [59]. The characteristic vibration peaks of Mg-Zn-Mo-O can still be found in the Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4, Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD, and Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD composites. So, the FT-IR spectra of resultant composites confirm that the structure of Mg-Zn-Mo-O remains unchanged. The FTIR spectrum of the prepared Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD composite contained the majority of characteristic signals of g-C3N4, and CQD indicating the presence in the ternary composite. Moreover, significant obvious peaks for the CQD and g-C3N4 can be detected in the composites, which is due to the high proportion of the CQD and g-C3N4 in the synthesized composites.

The surface morphology of prepared nanomaterials was examined by FESEM that are presented in Fig. 4. The FESEM images of the Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanostructures were investigated before and after calcination. The FESEM image before calcination show the sheet-like morphology. The characterized morphology of Mg-Zn-Mo-O after calcination changed to nano particles owing to the effect of thermal treatment. Indeed, the sheet-like morphology was changed under the calcination condition. Calcined products are mainly agglomerated and come in different shapes and sizes. The calcination process is a conventional and efficient method not only of removing the remnant organic materials but also of improving crystallinity. Obtaining highly active crystals through the calcination process remains a major challenge because high calcination temperature significantly decreases the surface area of the crystals by reducing porosity. The calcination process can affect the crystallinity, morphology, and size of structures. Applying the temperature to the synthesized sample can remove the organic compound of PVP which surrounded the resultant product. By thermal treatment, the PVP remove from resultant structures, and the connection between structures changes. This process led to the changing the morphology from sheet to nanoparticle [60]. The FESEM images of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 and Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD indicated decorated g-C3N4 on the Mg-Zn-Mo-O surface. In addition, the Mg-Zn-Mo-O and Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 samples which is composited with CQD cannot show CQD particles, probably because of the small size of CQD (commonly 2 – 10 nm) [61,62].

The energy dispersive x-ray (EDS) micrographs of the Mg-Zn-Mo-O and Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD nanomaterials display main signals for Mg-Zn-Mo-O (i.e., magnesium, zinc, oxygen, and molybdenum), g-C3N4 (i.e., carbon, and nitrogen), and CQD (i.e., carbon and oxygen). The elemental maps observed in Fig. 5a and b confirm the distribution of these elements over the whole prepared nanomaterials.

Fig. 6(a–d) show the resultant images of TEM and HR-TEM which confirm the presence of three components in the ternary nanocomposites. The amorphous parts in these images illustrate the CQD and g-C3N4 components. Also, the nanostructured Mg-Zn-Mo-O display on the surface of carbon-based matrix. The related crystal fringes for Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanoparticles specified in the Fig. 6d about 0.361 nm.

The synthesized nanostructures (Mg-Zn-Mo-O and Mg-Zn-Mo-O /g-C3N4/CQD) were evaluated to further insight into the surface properties by adsorption and desorption of nitrogen gas. Table 1 shows the surface area, average porosity volume, and pore size information. As we expected, improved surface properties of the new three-component nanocomposites allow the synthesized adsorbent to perform more efficiently in hydrogen storage.

The resultant active materials of bare Mg-Zn-Mo-O, Mg-Zn-Mo-O /g-C3N4, Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD and Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD nanocomposites are compared in Fig. 7 based on cyclic voltammograms. The cyclic voltammograms of the mentioned materials were provided in the 2.0 M KOH electrolyte and the potential range of − 1.0 to − 0.3 V, which was performed at a scanning rate of 0.1 Vs−1. This CV test system involves of three workings, reference (Ag/AgCl/KCl), and counter (Pt plate) electrodes. The cathodic peak is related to adsorption of hydrogen on active material and anodic peak can be attributed to reversible electrochemical hydrogen adsorption/desorption reaction occurring on the fabricated electrodes. By comparing the results of CV tests ( Table 2), we can see that the anodic current for binary Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 nanocomposites are higher than bare Mg-Zn-Mo-O and binary nanocomposites of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD. It is interesting to note that presence of Mg-Zn-Mo-O materials in combination with CQD and g-C3N4 constructions, can modify the electrochemical behaviors of electrode compounds. The two potential peaks in both anodic and cathodic administered for all samples noticeably reflected the required electrochemical characteristic for hydrogen storage. The cathodic peak is correlated to adsorption of hydrogen on active material and the anodic peak can be ascribed to reversible electrochemical hydrogen adsorption/desorption reaction happening on the invented electrodes [63].

The hydrogen storage capacity for resultant active materials were evaluated in the 2.0 M KOH electrolyte at current of 1 mA in three-electrode cell of working, reference (Ag/AgCl/KCl), and counter (Pt plate) electrodes, which present in Fig. 8. The discharge profile for Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanostructures shows in Fig. 8a from 1st to 15th cycles. The discharge capacity for Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanostructures in maximum is 112 mA h g−1. The binary nanocomposites of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 and Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD were analyzed in terms of hydrogen storage capacity for 15 cycles which illustrate in Fig. 8(b, c). The maximum capacity for Mg-Zn-Mo-O composites based on g-C3N4 and CQD are 250 and 162 mA h g−1 correspondingly. However, the ternary nanocomposites of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD show the capacity of hydrogen storage after 15 cycles in 202 mA h g−1 (Fig. 8d). The results confirm positive effect of g-C3N4 and CQD on the Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanostructures to alter the hydrogen storage capacity in alkaline medium. g-C3N4 with exceptional features of surface area and chemical resilience display suitable capability in the energy storage categories. The polymeric graphitic carbon nitride semiconductor rectifies the hydrogen sorption capacity in electrochemical methods through several mechanisms. (i) free metal-carbon nitride electro-catalyst as a basic effective point, (ii) it prohibits corrosion as a preservative factor, and (iii) carbon nitride has multiple triazine parts that can organize a possible joining with the metallic molecules as effective positions [48]. According to the results, the capacity of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 is higher than Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4/CQD due to covering the CQD on the Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 structures. The CQD can blocked the pores and intervals between layers. So, CQD did not show good dispersion on the surface of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 and blocked the pores. It seems that carbon quantum dots block the pore and layers of nanoparticle and g-C3N4 and inhibit the ion insertion in the structures [64,65].

The hydrogen storage capacity for Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD is better than pure Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanoparticles due to the rapid electron transfer in the CQD materials. The creating CQD with Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanoparticles can modification the capacity, because of the rich edge areas organized by small sized CQD and the appropriate conductivity correlated to Mg-Zn-Mo-O bond structures. The high performance of CQD based nanocomposites can be elucidating owing to the trap states formed which can adsorb charge carriers to upsurge the storage capacity [40]. This construction varying with cooperating CQD play an excessive role in electrochemical energy storage devices. The small size of CQD may be formed the electronic states due to presence of large amount of its atoms on the surface. These states can act as electron or hole traps which focusing at the surface. The surface of quantum dot is an exceedingly dynamic area, matched by chemical species which can have important effects on the of the quantum dots attributes. These effects can lead to creations of localized electronic states or extremely reactive sites, which are inclined to chemical and redox procedures [66].
The mechanism for hydrogen production and absorption in KOH electrolyte is as follows:

Based on Eq. (6) (known as Volmer), the water itself is the source of hydrogen leads to the creation of hydroxyl ions after transfer of electron.
(6) H2O + e- ↔ H + OH

According to the Eq. (7), the created hydrogen can be absorbed on the working electrode (XHads).
(7) X + H → XHads

Tefal and Heyrovsky equation known as Eqs. (8) and (9) recombine to provide H2, if the energy of hydrogen adsorption is lower than the released energy.
(8) XHads +H2O + e- → H2 + OH- + X
(9) 2H ↔ H2

In the discharge stage of hydrogen from active materials, the reaction of Eq. (10) occurs.
(10) XHads +XHads → H2 + 2X

Due to the Eq. (11) is discussed that Mo6+ is reduced to Mo5+ and then, hydrogen adsorption may balance the whole charge of Mg-Zn-Mo-O. The compound approved the organization of hydroxyl anions with a reduction of Mo6+to Mo5+ over the generation of bonds between molybdenum and hydrogen. [67].
(11) Mg-Zn-Mo(VI) -O + x H2O + x e - ↔ Mg-Zn-Mo(VI) 1−xMo(V)x-O -H(I) x+ x OH-
(12) (Mg-Zn-Mo -O)-Hads + (Mg-Zn-Mo -O)-Hads ↔ 2 Mg-Zn-Mo-O+ H2

Multiple potential plateaus in the discharge pattern (Fig. 8) can be due to the multiple hydrogen adsorption sites, which generated owing to the special structural defects, roughness, bonding, etc. long potential plateau can be observed due to the formation of stable chemical bonds between protons and oxygen in the oxide. The hydrogen storage in mixed metal oxides is a step-wise electrochemical process, manifesting different potential plateaus in the charge and discharge profiles. A step-wise electrochemical process produces various distinct sites for hydrogen sorption; on the surface, into the nanopores of bulk and/or into the interlayer [68].

The CQD as a component of ternary nanocomposites provided through green synthesis method via Salix aegyptiaca as a natural source. Also, synthesis of nanostructured Mg-Zn-Mo-O were conducted by two step method of precipitation and ion-exchange. The nanocomposites of Mg-Zn-Mo-O /g-C3N4 /CQD designed as active material for hydrogen storage by electrochemical method. The maximum discharge capacity after 15 cycles for Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 /CQD composites achieved about 202 mA h g−1 whereas the maximum capacity for nanocomposites of Mg-Zn-Mo-O/g-C3N4 and Mg-Zn-Mo-O/CQD were saved around 250 and 162 mA h g−1. The capacity of Mg-Zn-Mo-O nanoparticles is 112 mA h g−1. Correspondingly, the synergic effect of Mg-Zn-Mo-O on the support of CQD and g-C3N4 led to improving capacity for electrochemical hydrogen sorption in alkaline medium.

Fatemeh Karkeh-abadi: Software, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Maryam Ghiyasiyan-Arani: Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation, Investigation, Software, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Masoud Salavati-Niasari: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Data curation, Validation, Resources, Visualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to thank University of Kashan (Grant No. 159271/FK3) and Iran National Science Foundation (INSF: 97017837) for equipping access to the analysis and experimental instruments.
